title: Hand on chest: "You were doing your best"
subtitle: A three-minute embodiment practice for self-forgiveness about a past money choice.
excerpt: A three-minute embodiment practice for self-forgiveness about a past money choice. One hand flat on the centre of the chest, one short sentence said to the version of you who made the choice. Used when shame about a past money decision is loud and unproductive.
sourceNotes: Self-compassion practice with a hand-on-heart anchor is documented across the somatic-therapy lineage (Peter Levine, Pat Ogden) and the self-compassion research literature (Kristin Neff, 2003 onward). Theme adapted from Day 5 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Release shame about past mistakes".

content.text: A three-minute embodiment practice for self-forgiveness about a past money choice. One hand flat on the centre of the chest, one short sentence said to the version of you who made the choice. The hand is the anchor; the sentence is the work.
Sit somewhere quiet. Both feet flat on the floor. Place your right hand flat on the centre of the chest, fingers gently spread. Feel the weight of the hand against the chest.
Bring to mind the past money choice you are working with. The specific spend, the specific year, the specific version of you who made the decision. Don't argue with the choice; just see her.
Say to her, out loud or in your mind:

attrs.quote: You were doing your best with what you had.

content.text: Say it three times. The hand stays on the chest. The slow even pressure of the hand is part of the practice, the body hears the words and the hand at the same time.
Let the hand stay on the chest for one slow minute longer. Notice the breath under the hand. The choice happened; the version of you who made it is allowed to be carried with care, not litigated.
When you take the hand away, leave the warmth where the hand was. That warmth is the practice landing.

Hand-on-heart self-touch sits in two practice lines. The body-based therapy world (Peter Levine, Pat Ogden) uses touch as a route to a calm body. The research side (Kristin Neff, University of Texas, from 2003) names hand-on-heart as a core self-kindness practice. The money self-forgiveness theme is adapted from Day 5 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). That day works on past money shame as a loop.
